primary_diagnosis.name: Axial psoriatic arthritis flare (sacroiliitis) — cauda equina to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Axial psoriatic arthritis causes inflammation of the sacroiliac joints and spine, producing inflammatory-pattern back pain (prolonged morning stiffness, pain better with movement and worse at rest), bilateral SI/buttock pain, protective paraspinal spasm and severe fatigue. A severe flare can cause near-total axial rigidity and immobility, mimicking cauda equina syndrome, cord compression, acute disc herniation and vertebral collapse. Reassuring features are a negative cauda-equina screen (no saddle anaesthesia, no sphincter disturbance), no new leg weakness or below-knee sciatica, and intact neurology on exam. However, telehealth cannot perform the spinal/neurological examination or MRI, so cauda equina cannot be fully excluded remotely: the correct action is urgent in-person review (± MRI) with explicit cauda-equina safety-netting, optimising the anti-inflammatory (a prescription NSAID such as meloxicam if no contraindication) rather than a remote injection, plus fall-safety for an isolated patient, and 000 for any saddle numbness, sphincter disturbance or new leg weakness.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe back pain with immobility demands a cauda-equina screen first.
Inflammatory-pattern back pain on known axial PsA is most likely.
Against — inflammatory pattern with prolonged morning stiffness.
Negative cauda-equina screen, no radiculopathy, inflammatory pattern.
Now low probability but retained as a safety-net (unexaminable remotely).

differential.diagnosis: Axial psoriatic arthritis flare — cauda equina safety-netted
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: axial PsA flare needing urgent in-person review (± MRI), NSAID optimisation, fall-safety, and cauda-equina safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Saddle numbness, bladder/bowel incontinence or retention (cauda equina)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cauda equina is a time-critical surgical emergency; delay causes permanent damage.

red_flags.description: New leg weakness or progressive neurological deficit
High fall risk from axial rigidity in an isolated home with hazards
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe rigidity/pain while alone with floor hazards risks falls.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening/safety-netting saddle anaesthesia and sphincter dysfunction.
Not recognising the patient has maxed OTC NSAIDs before recommending more.
Not addressing the isolated fall risk and floor hazards.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Negative (no saddle anaesthesia, sphincter disturbance or new leg weakness)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports an axial inflammatory flare with urgent in-person review and cauda-equina safety-netting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Spinal and neurological examination (Schober, perianal sensation, power)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen cauda equina; arrange urgent in-person review (± MRI) and optimise a prescription NSAID (if no contraindication); organise fall-safety; escalate to 000 for saddle numbness, sphincter disturbance or new leg weakness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing remotely without a cauda-equina screen could miss a surgical emergency; more NSAIDs beyond limits risks toxicity.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: NSAIDs are first-line for axial inflammatory disease; persistent disease needs rheumatology review and biologic consideration; MRI assesses active sacroiliitis.
Saddle anaesthesia, sphincter disturbance or progressive leg weakness require emergency imaging and surgical assessment.
